agent_output:
```json
{
  "summary": {
    "verdict": "BLOCK",
    "one_liner": "Wildcard Stripe SDK version and pinned vulnerable jsonwebtoken in a payment service are unacceptable production risks."
  },

  "critical_findings": [
    {
      "title": "Wildcard version on Stripe SDK allows untested breaking changes in production",
      "risk": "stripe: \"*\" means any npm install, deploy, or CI rebuild can silently pull a new major version of the payment processor SDK. Stripe regularly ships breaking changes across majors (API version changes, removed methods, altered webhook signatures). A routine deploy could break all payment processing with zero code changes.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "package.json — dependencies.stripe",
      "reproduction": "1. Run npm install today, note stripe@14.x installed. 2. Wait for stripe@15.0.0 release. 3. Run npm install again — new major pulled automatically. 4. Payment creation calls fail due to changed API signatures.",
      "fix": "Pin to a specific minor range: \"stripe\": \"^14.21.0\" (or whichever version is currently tested). Lock with package-lock.json committed to repo. Never use * for any dependency, especially one that moves money.",
      "time_to_fix": "5 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "All payment flows — charge, refund, webhook verification"
    },
    {
      "title": "jsonwebtoken 8.5.1 has known CVEs allowing JWT forgery",
      "risk": "jsonwebtoken 8.5.1 is vulnerable to CVE-2022-23529 (remote code execution via crafted key) and CVE-2022-23539/23540/23541 (insecure key handling allowing token forgery). In a payment service, JWT forgery means an attacker can forge auth tokens and initiate unauthorized payments or access other users' payment data.",
      "severity": "Critical",
      "location": "package.json — dependencies.jsonwebtoken",
      "reproduction": "1. Attacker crafts a malicious JWK with a toString override. 2. If the service passes user-controlled input as the secretOrPublicKey, attacker achieves RCE. 3. Even without RCE, weak algorithm checks allow algorithm confusion attacks to forge valid tokens.",
      "fix": "Upgrade to jsonwebtoken >=9.0.0 which patches all known CVEs. Change to: \"jsonwebtoken\": \"^9.0.0\". Audit all jwt.verify() call sites to ensure algorithms are explicitly specified in the options object.",
      "time_to_fix": "30 minutes (upgrade + verify call sites)",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Authentication and authorization for all payment endpoints"
    },
    {
      "title": "Unnecessary left-pad dependency is a supply chain attack vector on a payment service",
      "risk": "left-pad is a trivial one-liner (string padding) that infamously broke the entire npm ecosystem in 2016 when unpublished. Including it in a payment service adds an unnecessary supply chain attack surface — a compromised or hijacked trivial package gets full access to the payment service runtime, including Stripe keys and database credentials. Node.js has had native String.prototype.padStart() since v8.",
      "severity": "High",
      "location": "package.json — dependencies.left-pad",
      "reproduction": "1. left-pad maintainer account is compromised. 2. Malicious version published that exfiltrates process.env (containing STRIPE_SECRET_KEY, DB credentials). 3. Next npm install pulls compromised version. 4. All secrets exfiltrated on service startup.",
      "fix": "Remove left-pad entirely. Replace all usages with native String.prototype.padStart(). Example: instead of leftPad(str, 10, '0'), use str.padStart(10, '0').",
      "time_to_fix": "15 minutes",
      "acceptance_criteria_affected": "Service security posture and supply chain integrity"
    }
  ],

  "important_issues": [
    {
      "title": "moment.js is deprecated and oversized for a microservice",
      "description": "Moment.js is officially in maintenance mode (no new features, deprecated by its own maintainers). It adds ~300KB to the bundle, is mutable by default (leading to subtle date bugs), and lacks tree-shaking. Replace with date-fns or dayjs.",
      "location": "package.json — dependencies.moment"
    },
    {
      "title": "crypto-random-string 5.0.0 is ESM-only, likely breaks CommonJS require()",
      "description": "crypto-random-string v5 is a pure ESM module. If the payment service uses CommonJS (require()), this will throw ERR_REQUIRE_ESM at runtime. Either pin to v4.x (last CJS version) or ensure the entire service is ESM.",
      "location": "package.json — dependencies.crypto-random-string"
    },
    {
      "title": "pg ^8.0.0 range is too wide — spans 4+ years of major changes",
      "description": "^8.0.0 allows any 8.x version. pg has had behavioral changes across 8.x minors (e.g., BigInt handling, SSL defaults). For a payment service, pin closer to the tested version, e.g., ^8.13.0.",
      "location": "package.json — dependencies.pg"
    },
    {
      "title": "No package-lock.json mentioned — builds are non-reproducible",
      "description": "With ranges like ^4.18.0 and *, without a committed lockfile every install can produce different dependency trees. Payment services must have reproducible builds. Ensure package-lock.json is committed and CI uses npm ci.",
      "location": "package.json (project root)"
    }
  ],

  "suggestions": [
    "Add a engines field to enforce minimum Node.js version (>=18 for native fetch, padStart, etc.)",
    "Add npm overrides or audit scripts to CI to catch future CVEs automatically",
    "Consider adding express-rate-limit or similar to protect payment endpoints from abuse"
  ],

  "production_readiness": {
    "deployment_risks": "Any deploy that runs npm install will get unpredictable dependency versions due to wildcard and wide ranges. Stripe SDK could break between deploys with zero code changes. ESM/CJS mismatch in crypto-random-string could cause immediate crash on deploy.",
    "rollback_capability": "Rollback is safe IF you roll back node_modules or use a lockfile. Without a lockfile, rollback may pull different deps than the original deploy.",
    "backward_compatibility": "N/A — this is a dependency manifest, not an API change.",
    "monitoring_coverage": "No monitoring dependencies present. Payment services should have structured logging, metrics, and health checks.",
    "breaking_changes": {
      "api_breaking": false,
      "db_destructive": false,
      "shared_library": false,
      "auth_security": true,
      "data_pipeline": false,
      "config_changes": false,
      "details": [
        "jsonwebtoken 8.5.1 has known auth bypass vulnerabilities that directly impact payment service security"
      ]
    },
    "safe_to_deploy": false,
    "rollback_plan": "Do not deploy until Stripe is pinned, jsonwebtoken is upgraded to >=9.0.0, and left-pad is removed. Ensure package-lock.json is committed before any deploy."
  },

  "escalations": [],

  "confidence": {
    "level": 97,
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Stripe wildcard version risk is objective and well-documented",
      "jsonwebtoken CVEs are published and verified",
      "left-pad supply chain risk is historically proven"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Cannot verify ESM/CJS issue without seeing the service's module system configuration",
      "Cannot assess actual jwt.verify() call sites without seeing application code"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "Service is deployed via standard npm install workflow",
      "No additional lockfile or shrinkwrap mechanism exists outside this manifest",
      "Service handles real financial transactions in production"
    ]
  }
}
```

content.thinking:
Let me analyze this package.json for a payment processing microservice and produce my quality review.

Classification: `none` — these are dependency-level issues with well-known fixes.

Let me form hypotheses:

1. `stripe: "*"` — wildcard version for payment processor SDK is extremely dangerous
2. `jsonwebtoken: 8.5.1` — known CVEs in older versions
3. `left-pad` — unnecessary dependency, supply chain risk
4. `moment` — deprecated library
5. `crypto-random-string: 5.0.0` — ESM-only module, may break CommonJS


6. Now I'm compiling the findings into a structured report with severity levels and remediation steps for each issue.
